retrieved_docs.title: Hyperammonemia -- Pathophysiology -- Role of Ammonia in Neurotoxicity
retrieved_docs.content: The exact mechanism by which ammonia results in CNS damage has not been established. It has been hypothesized that various alterations in the neurotransmitter system are responsible for the neuronal damage seen in both acute and chronic hyperammonemia. Animal studies have shown that acute ammonia intoxication in vitro results in elevated extracellular levels of glutamate in the brain. This results in activation of the N-methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptors, which causes decreased phosphorylation of protein kinase C that results in the activation of Na/K-ATPase. The resultant ATP depletion is responsible for ammonia toxicity and is the most probable cause of seizures in acute hyperammonemia. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Ammonia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Ammonia production occurs in all tissues of the body during the metabolism of a variety of compounds. Ammonia is produced by metabolizing amino acids and other compounds containing nitrogen. Ammonia exists as an ammonium ion (NH4 + ) at the physiological pH. It is produced in our body mainly by transamination followed by deamination from biogenic amines, from amino groups of nitrogenous bases like purine and pyrimidine, and in the intestine by intestinal bacterial flora through urease action on urea. Ammonia disposal takes place primarily by the hepatic formation of urea. The blood level of ammonia must remain very low because even slightly elevated concentrations (hyperammonemia) are toxic to the central nervous system. A metabolic mechanism exists by which nitrogen is moved from peripheral tissues to the liver for its ultimate disposal as urea while maintaining low circulating ammonia levels.

retrieved_docs.title: Portal-Systemic Encephalopathy(Archived) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The most important causative factor of hepatic encephalopathy is increased ammonia level in the blood. Other known neurotoxins include short-chain fatty acids, mercaptans, tyramine, octopamine, manganese, and gamma-aminobutyric acid (GABA). Determining the blood ammonia level is complicated due to a variety of reactions taking place in multiple organs. Metabolism of ammonia takes place in the liver, gastrointestinal tract, muscles, kidney, and brain. The enormous amount of ammonia produced by amino acid metabolism is captured by the urea cycle and does not contribute to blood ammonia levels under normal conditions. The primary site of production of ammonia is the gastrointestinal tract. The three main mechanisms of production of ammonia in the gut are hydrolysis of urea by bacterial urease, bacterial protein deamination, and glutamine metabolism in the intestinal mucosa.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperammonemia -- Pathophysiology -- Role of Ammonia in Neurotoxicity
retrieved_docs.content: Another consequence of acute hyperammonemia is cerebral edema. This occurs primarily due to the swelling of astrocytes, which are the only cells involved in ammonia detoxification in the brain. Proposed mechanisms for astrocyte swelling include altered water and K+ metabolism in the astrocytes, activation of tumor suppressor protein p53, increased uptake of certain compounds including pyruvate, lactate, and glutamine and decreased uptake of ketone bodies, glutamate, and free glucose. Cerebral edema leads to raised intracranial pressure, which may result in brain herniation. [13] Chronic hyperammonemia results in two major pathological changes, both of which result in increased inhibitory neurotransmission.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperammonemia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperammonemia is a metabolic condition characterized by the raised levels of ammonia, a nitrogen-containing compound. Normal levels of ammonia in the body vary according to age. Hyperammonemia can result from various congenital and acquired conditions in which it may be the principal toxin. Hyperammonemia may also occur as a part of other disorders that involve various other metabolic abnormalities. Normally, ammonia is produced in the colon and small intestine from where it is transported to the liver to be converted to urea via the urea cycle. Urea, a water-soluble compound, can then be excreted via the kidneys. Ammonia levels rise if the liver is unable to metabolize this toxic compound as a result of an enzymatic defect or hepatocellular damage. The levels may also rise if portal blood is diverted to the systemic circulation, bypassing the liver, or there is increased production of ammonia due to an infection with certain microorganisms. [1] [2] Hyperammonemia in adults is most commonly related to cirrhotic liver disease in 90% of the cases. This metabolic abnormality, however, can be seen in numerous other disorders that need to be understood. Ammonia is a potent neurotoxin, and elevated levels in the blood can cause neurological signs and symptoms that may be acute or chronic, depending on the underlying abnormality. Hyperammonemia should be recognized early and treated immediately to prevent the development of life-threatening complications such as cerebral edema and brain herniation. Treatment strategies vary according to etiology.

retrieved_docs.title: Neomycin -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The pathogenesis of hepatic coma frequently stems from underlying liver disease, resulting in elevated ammonia levels in the bloodstream. Ammonia can traverse the blood-brain barriers at high levels, thereby giving rise to numerous neurological manifestations associated with hepatic coma. Furthermore, ammonia within the brain instigates heightened levels of glutamine and lactate, contributing to neuronal edema. [6] Therefore, therapeutic approaches are focused on reducing ammonia levels through the attenuation of ammonia production or the enhancement of ammonia excretion.

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatic Encephalopathy -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The neuropathology of hepatic encephalopathy was first described in 1949, but our understanding of its exact causes and mechanisms remains incomplete. [1] A central factor in the pathogenesis of hepatic encephalopathy is the role of elevated serum ammonia levels. Colonic bacteria and mucosal enzymes break down dietary proteins, releasing ammonia in the gastrointestinal tract. This ammonia is normally absorbed into the portal circulation, where the liver converts it into urea through the urea cycle. However, in cases of hepatic failure or systemic shunting, ammonia can accumulate and bypass the liver, entering the systemic circulation. This buildup of ammonia, known as hyperammonemia, disrupts neuronal function and contributes to encephalopathy. Ammonia is one of several neurotoxic substances that impair excitatory neurotransmission. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Urea Cycle -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperammonemia is toxic to the brain and leads to encephalopathy, which can manifest as cerebral edema, vomiting, blurred vision, asterixis, and seizures. Excess ammonia will also result in the increased formation of glutamine. Recall that glutamine synthetase uses the reactants NH and glutamate to yield glutamine. Glutamate is an excitatory neurotransmitter, and therefore decreased levels of glutamate will cause depressed neural activity, which manifests as lethargy or a comatose state. In addition, excess ammonia hinders the TCA cycle by causing alpha-ketoglutarate to form glutamate. Here we will discuss the most common urea cycle disorders:

retrieved_docs.title: Ammonia Toxicity -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Ammonia is a colorless irritant gas with a pungent order readily soluble in water to generate ammonium ions. [1] Ammonia is a natural by-product in the human body as an intermediate in several metabolic reactions primarily involving amino acid synthesis. [2] It also gets produced in the human gut due to various enzymatic actions of bacteria. [3] However, due to the highly toxic nature of ammonia, it is quickly metabolized into urea in the liver by the urea cycle and excreted by the kidneys. [4] [5] The blood ammonia level in a healthy adult ranges from 15 to 45 micrograms/dL. [6] Ammonia toxicity occurs when the ammonia content in the blood supersedes the liver’s capacity to eliminate it; this could be a result of either overproduction, such as in congenital hyperammonemia, or under-elimination, such as in liver cirrhosis. [7] [8] This topic focuses on the various means by which the human body suffers exposure to external sources of ammonia and the multiple mechanisms of its toxicity.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Urea Cycle -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The liver catabolizes toxic ammonia into urea, as shown in figure 1.  This energy-dependent process occurs only within the liver’s mitochondria and cytoplasm. When this process is not working efficiently, toxic ammonia (NH3) accumulates within the body and may elicit clinical manifestations such as lethargy, slurred speech, cerebral edema, and asterixis. Here, we will review the underlying physiology of the urea cycle. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Hepatic Encephalopathy -- Treatment / Management -- Ammonia Levels Management
retrieved_docs.content: Lactulose: Lactulose works through multiple mechanisms to reduce blood ammonia levels. When bacteria in the gut break down lactulose, it creates an acidic environment that promotes the conversion of ammonia (NH 3 ) to ammonium (NH 4 + ), reducing the diffusion of NH 3 into the bloodstream. Additionally, lactulose promotes the diffusion of NH 3 from the bloodstream into the gut, where it is converted to NH 4 + . Furthermore, lactulose also exerts an osmotic effect in the colon, promoting distention and peristalsis, which facilitates the excretion of NH 3 . Through these combined mechanisms, lactulose effectively reduces blood ammonia levels of NH 3 . Typically, lactulose is administered at a dose of 20 to 30 grams, 2 to 4 times per day, to achieve at least 2 to 3 soft stools daily. In cases where oral administration is not possible, lactulose can be administered as an enema. About 70% to 80% of patients with hepatic encephalopathy show improvement with lactulose treatment. [37] [38] [39] While lactitol is available in certain countries, it is not approved for use in the United States.

retrieved_docs.title: Portal-Systemic Encephalopathy(Archived) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The major cause of encephalopathy in all three types is the failure of ammonia to get metabolized into a less toxic state by the urea cycle in portal hepatocytes in the liver, either due to liver dysfunction or the development of a portosystemic shunt. The astrocyte cell membrane is highly permeable to the ammonium ion. A low concentration of ammonia can cross the blood-brain barrier reaching the brain and gets deposited there. The deposition of ammonia in the brain causes a neuropsychiatric syndrome. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Ammonia Toxicity -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Gas leaks from the production, storage, or transportation facilities of ammonia. Hence, people who work in such facilities are at an increased risk for inhalational and dermal exposure. [9] [10] A high ammonia level may be present in the air after applying ammonia-containing fertilizers in the soil. [11] Hence, the farmers who rely on ammonia-based fertilizers are at an increased risk for inhalational exposure. Household and industrial cleaners contain 5% to 25% of ammonia in dissolved form. Hence, the residents, especially children of such households, are at an increased risk for accidental or suicidal ingestion exposure. The spillage of such products can lead to significant inhalational exposure as well. [12] Ammonia is produced by decaying manure. Hence, farmers working in animal confinement buildings are at an increased risk of inhalation. [13] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Ammonia Toxicity -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: The Agency for Toxic Substances and Disease Registry provides the following recommendations to the general public for avoiding ammonia exposure: Ammonia-based household cleaner use requires well-ventilated rooms. Avoid storing ammonia-based cleaners in glass bottles, as breakage could lead to severe exposure. Wear proper clothing and use eye protection while using ammonia-based cleaners. Keep ammonia-based cleaners out of reach of children. Avoid visiting farming areas where ammonia-based fertilizers are applied immediately after their application. Avoid visiting animal confinement buildings without the use of proper personal protective equipment. They also recommend that workers who use or apply ammonia in farming always follow proper safety precautions mentioned on the equipment and use personal protective equipment wherever necessary.

retrieved_docs.title: Portal-Systemic Encephalopathy(Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Due to the shunt, ammonia, and other neurotoxins that usually get metabolized in the liver, bypass and cross the blood-brain barrier leading to the accumulation of neurotoxins in the brain, causing cognitive and psychomotor disturbances. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2863
retrieved_docs.content: Acute kidney failure (فشل كلوي حاد) is clinically associated with dehydration (التجفاف) and with abnormal mental states (حالة نفسية غير طبيعية). When renal function declines sharply, the condition can give rise to extrarenal blood nitrogen accumulation (نيتروجينية الدم خارج الكلوية), reflecting the buildup of nitrogenous waste products that the kidneys are no longer able to clear. These interrelated clinical features highlight the importance of recognizing fluid deficits and neuropsychiatric changes as potential indicators of acute kidney injury and of monitoring nitrogen waste levels to guide timely management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2015
retrieved_docs.content: Coma, stupor, and brain damage are clinical manifestations that frequently co‑occur with عَوَز الأكسجين, indicating that insufficient oxygen supply to the brain can lead to these severe neurological states.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 842
retrieved_docs.content: of coma, stupor, and brain damage, indicating that this form of oxygen support is frequently associated with severe neurological impairment. Collectively, these relationships demonstrate how specific elements—manganese, phosphorus, and oxygen‑related compounds—are integrated into pharmaceutical products, organized within a chemical hierarchy, and linked to both therapeutic applications and adverse clinical contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 2 -- Community 912
retrieved_docs.content: The knowledge base classifies mineral supplements as a broad category of nutritional products that deliver essential elements such as calcium (vápník), phosphorus (fosfor), boron (bor), molybdenum (molybden), and the Arabic‑named element عَوَز الأكسجين. Within mineral supplements, calcium supplements form a defined subclass and comprise specific calcium salts—including calcium glubionate, calcium lactate, calcium levulinate, and citrát vápenatý—as well as calcium antacid, which provides calcium ions for gastric acid neutralization. All these entities are organized under the parent taxonomy “Elements, Ions and Simple Compounds,” which groups individual mineral elements and their simple compounds. Clinically, oxygen deprivation (عَوَز الأكسجين) is linked to severe neurological conditions; insufficient cerebral oxygen can precipitate stupor, coma, and irreversible brain damage. Recognizing the relationship between oxygen deprivation and these neurologic outcomes is critical for diagnosis, prevention, and therapeutic intervention, while mineral and calcium supplementation supports nutritional and therapeutic needs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 157
retrieved_docs.content: and primary excessive daytime sleepiness. The non‑infectious delirium/brain disorder (SMQ) similarly features apathy (اللامبالاة), attention impairment, reduced concentration, and primary excessive daytime sleepiness. Consequently, excessive daytime sleepiness serves as a linking symptom across arousal disturbances in both encephalopathic and delirium‑like SMQ categories, underscoring its clinical relevance for clinicians managing non‑infectious brain disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 169
retrieved_docs.content: pathologies include encephalitis, اعتلال دماغي أيضي (metabolic brain disease), انْضِغاطُ الدِّماغ (brain atrophy), أوديما دماغية (cerebral edema) and its subtype Traumatisch hersenoedeem (traumatic brain edema), اعتلال دماغي (brain disease) and its toxic form اعتلال دماغي سمي (toxic brain disease). Acute brain injury (إصابة حادة في الدماغ) is an instance of اعتلال دماغي. Therapeutically, interventions are performed directly on the brain, including العلاج بالتخليج الكهربي (electrocoagulation therapy) and the neurosurgical (جراحة الأعصاب) procedure of insertion, replacement, or removal of an extracranial ventricular shunt. This procedure encompasses Shunt of cerebral ventricle to extracranial site, with a child entity التحويلة البطينية البريتونية and a synonymous entity Ventricular shunt to nasopharynx, and is linked to the anatomic site إحصار عصبي عضلي (muscular neuro‑blockade) within the nervous system. retrieved_docs.content: The elemental group also includes boron, fluoride, phosphorus, molybdenum, zinc and oxygen. Clinical observations within the community associate severe neurological states—coma, stupor and brain damage—with the concurrent use of supplemental oxygen therapy, reflecting the frequent need for oxygen support in critical neurological conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 1 -- Community 60
retrieved_docs.content: This hierarchical knowledge base connects each causative agent to its specific poisoning type, situates those poisonings within larger accidental or unspecified psychoactive poisoning frameworks, and delineates the spectrum of organic mental disorders and intoxication syndromes that may arise, thereby aiding clinicians in agent identification, poisoning classification, and anticipation of associated neuropsychiatric manifestations for optimal patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2126
retrieved_docs.content: Transient cerebral ischemia is characterized by a transitory clinical course, indicating that the neurological deficits associated with this brief interruption of cerebral blood flow are temporary and typically resolve quickly without permanent damage.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 84
retrieved_docs.content: as نوبة بداية عدم الحركة المعممة, originating in the cerebrum (velký mozek). Mefobarbital is an effective therapeutic option for controlling these seizures. Hypoxic conditions (عَوَز الأكسجين, oxygen deficiency) frequently accompany coma, stupor, and brain damage, underscoring the need for vigilant monitoring and management of oxygenation in individuals with seizure disorders or toxic exposures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 2261
retrieved_docs.content: Carbon monoxide, referred to in Czech as oxid uhelnatý, is classified both as a gasotransmitter and as an antimetabolite. As a gasotransmitter, it functions as a small, endogenously produced gaseous signaling molecule that can modulate vascular tone, neurotransmission, and inflammatory responses. Its antimetabolite properties allow it to interfere with normal metabolic pathways, for example by binding to heme‑containing enzymes and altering cellular respiration. Exposure to carbon monoxide is directly associated with carbon‑monoxide poisoning (التسمم بأول أكسيد الكربون), a clinical syndrome characterized by hypoxic injury, neurological deficits, and potentially fatal outcomes. Recognizing carbon monoxide’s dual role as a physiological messenger and a toxic agent is essential for accurate diagnosis, prevention, and management of poisoning cases.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 726
retrieved_docs.content: Elements, Ions and Simple Compounds is presented as a parent category that includes the elements bor (boron) and molybden (molybdenum) as well as the condition عَوَز الأكسجين (oxygen deficiency or hypoxia). The presence of عَوَز الأكسجين is noted to co‑occur with severe neurological manifestations, specifically coma, stupor, and brain damage. This relationship underscores the clinical relevance of elemental and compound deficiencies, particularly oxygen deficiency, in the development of critical brain injury and altered consciousness states.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2621
retrieved_docs.content: اعتلال كبيبات الكلى (renal glomerulopathy) is a disorder affecting the glomeruli of the kidneys, and it can progress to اليوريمية (uremia), a condition characterized by the accumulation of nitrogenous waste products in the blood due to impaired renal filtration. This connection highlights that damage to the kidney’s filtering units may lead to systemic toxicity, emphasizing the need for early identification and treatment of glomerular disease to prevent the development of uremic complications.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1995
retrieved_docs.content: Dehydration (التجفاف) is clinically associated with the development of acute kidney injury (فشل كلوي حاد). In turn, acute kidney injury is clinically linked to low blood volume (قلة حجم الدم), reflecting the importance of adequate intravascular volume in preserving renal function. Acute kidney injury also encompasses the condition of extracellular blood nitrogen (نيتروجينية الدم خارج الكلوية), indicating that disturbances in nitrogenous waste handling outside the kidneys are a relevant component of its clinical spectrum. Understanding these interconnections—how dehydration can precipitate acute kidney injury, how hypovolemia contributes to its progression, and how extracellular nitrogen abnormalities manifest—helps clinicians identify risk factors, monitor patients, and implement timely interventions to protect renal function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 706
retrieved_docs.content: اللامبالاة (apathy) is identified as a clinical feature of الهذيان/الاعتلال الدماغي غير المعدي (SMQ) (delirium/non‑organic brain syndrome). The syndrome itself is classified under خلل في الانتباه (attention disorder) and under ضعف التركيز (concentration impairment). Thus, attention deficits and reduced concentration are core aspects of delirium/non‑organic brain syndrome, and apathy may appear as one of its manifestations. Recognizing these interrelated signs supports more accurate assessment and management of patients with cognitive and attentional disturbances.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1906
retrieved_docs.content: تَشَمَّعٌ كُحولِيّ, a specific form of تَشَمُّع كَبِدي, is clinically associated with فرطُ بوتاسيوم الدَّم. This connection indicates that individuals with alcoholic encephalopathy may exhibit elevated blood potassium levels, underscoring the importance of monitoring electrolyte disturbances when managing hepatic encephalopathy.
